questionPatterns: Tell me about the hardest bug you've had to debug.
Walk me through your process when you can't immediately figure out why something is broken.
evaluationCriteria: describes a specific technical bug rather than a vague generality
shows a logical debugging process: isolate, hypothesize, test
reflects on the root cause, not just the fix

concepts: how they communicated and resolved it
self-awareness about their own role in the conflict
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a technical approach.
How do you typically handle conflict in a team setting?
evaluationCriteria: gives a concrete specific example rather than a hypothetical
shows constructive resolution, not just 'I was right'
reflects some self-awareness about their own contribution to the friction

concepts: communication before or after the deadline
what changed afterward to prevent recurrence
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you missed a deadline. What happened?
How do you handle a project falling behind schedule?
evaluationCriteria: takes genuine ownership rather than only blaming external factors
identifies a real specific cause
describes how the delay was communicated
describes a concrete change in approach afterward

concepts: specific decision from a real project
what they learned and would apply going forward
questionPatterns: Looking back at one of your projects, what technical decision would you make differently today?
What's something you built that you'd architect differently now?
evaluationCriteria: names a real specific decision from an actual project
shows genuine reflection rather than defensiveness
explains why the original decision seemed reasonable at the time
connects the lesson to how they would approach similar decisions now

questionPatterns: You have three urgent tasks and limited time. How do you decide what to do first?
Describe a time you had to prioritize ruthlessly to meet a deadline.
evaluationCriteria: describes a concrete method rather than simply working harder
considers impact, urgency, or dependencies
gives a real example with actual tradeoffs
shows awareness of what was deprioritized and why

concepts: coordinating others or driving scope and direction
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you took the lead on something without being asked to.
Describe a project where you drove the direction rather than just executing tasks.
evaluationCriteria: gives a genuine example of initiative
explains why they decided to take ownership
describes what leading actually looked like in practice
states a real outcome rather than only intent

concepts: initial reaction versus how they processed it
separating personal feelings from useful feedback
concrete change made as a result
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you received critical feedback on your code or work. How did you respond?
How do you typically react when someone points out a flaw in your approach?
evaluationCriteria: gives a real instance rather than a hypothetical
shows they did not immediately become defensive
evaluates the feedback objectively
describes an actual behavior change resulting from it

concepts: specific technology or skill learned under time pressure
how the knowledge was applied immediately
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you had to learn a new technology quickly to finish a project.
How do you approach ramping up on something completely unfamiliar?
evaluationCriteria: names a specific real technology or skill and timeframe
describes an actual learning method
shows ability to identify what needed to be learned first
connects the learning directly to shipping or solving something

questionPatterns: Tell me about a time the requirements for something you built were vague or kept changing.
How do you handle building something when the client or stakeholder isn't sure exactly what they want?
evaluationCriteria: gives a concrete example
describes proactively clarifying rather than guessing silently
makes reasonable assumptions when complete clarity is impossible
shows how assumptions were validated

questionPatterns: What do you focus on when reviewing someone else's code?
How do you give critical feedback on code without it feeling personal?
evaluationCriteria: names concrete review priorities beyond formatting
frames feedback constructively
distinguishes objective technical concerns from personal preference
shows self-awareness about tone when giving criticism

concepts: specific disagreement with reasoning on both sides
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision made by the team.
How do you handle being overruled on something you believe is the wrong technical call?
evaluationCriteria: gives a real example with clear reasoning
shows they made their case using evidence rather than ego
demonstrates ability to consider the opposing position
can disagree and commit when the final decision is reasonable

questionPatterns: How do you stay current with changes in the technologies you work with?
What's something new you learned recently and how did you apply it?
evaluationCriteria: names specific real habits or resources
connects learning to actual application
shows selectivity rather than chasing every new technology
avoids generic non-answers

questionPatterns: Which project are you most proud of, and why?
What's the most technically challenging thing you've built?
evaluationCriteria: clearly articulates their own contribution
identifies a genuine technical or design challenge
explains important decisions rather than only listing technologies
can go deeper when probed

questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you were confident about a technical approach and turned out to be wrong.
How do you know when to abandon an approach you've already invested time in?
evaluationCriteria: gives a genuine specific example
identifies evidence that challenged their assumption
does not hide behind sunk-cost thinking
describes concretely how they corrected course

questionPatterns: This role requires technologies you haven't used in production yet. How would you approach ramping up?
What makes you confident you can become productive with an unfamiliar technology?
evaluationCriteria: doesn't overclaim experience they don't have
provides a credible specific learning approach
identifies transferable experience
supports confidence with previous evidence of learning

questionPatterns: Tell me about something that failed because of a decision you made.
Describe a professional mistake that was primarily your responsibility.
evaluationCriteria: takes clear ownership without excessive blame-shifting
explains the actual impact of the failure
describes immediate corrective action
identifies a systemic improvement that reduced recurrence risk

questionPatterns: Tell me about a time several important tasks competed for your attention.
How would you handle two stakeholders who both say their task is the highest priority?
evaluationCriteria: doesn't simply attempt everything simultaneously
uses explicit criteria to prioritize
communicates constraints and tradeoffs
re-evaluates priorities when circumstances change

questionPatterns: Tell me about a project where the requirements changed significantly after development had started.
How do you respond when a client requests a major change close to a deadline?
evaluationCriteria: assesses the impact before immediately accepting or rejecting the change
communicates consequences clearly
looks for incremental or lower-cost alternatives
shows adaptability without ignoring engineering constraints

concepts: communicating technical topics to non-technical people
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you had to explain a technical problem to someone without a technical background.
How would you tell a client that their requested feature cannot be delivered by the original deadline?
evaluationCriteria: avoids unnecessary technical jargon
explains impact and options rather than only the technical problem
communicates bad news early rather than hiding it
demonstrates empathy for the stakeholder's actual goal

questionPatterns: Imagine a production feature you deployed suddenly stops working for users. What do you do?
Tell me about a serious issue you encountered after deployment and how you handled it.
evaluationCriteria: prioritizes reducing user impact before searching endlessly for the perfect root cause
uses logs, reproduction, monitoring, or rollback systematically
communicates status appropriately
distinguishes immediate mitigation from long-term prevention

questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you convinced others to adopt an idea when you weren't the decision-maker.
How would you convince a team to change a technical approach they are already comfortable with?
evaluationCriteria: uses evidence or demonstrated results rather than relying only on confidence
actively understands objections
adapts the proposal based on valid feedback
doesn't confuse influence with forcing agreement

topic: Making a Decision With Incomplete Information
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you had to make an important decision without having all the information you wanted.
How do you avoid analysis paralysis when there isn't an obviously correct technical choice?
evaluationCriteria: identifies what information is actually critical
makes assumptions explicit
balances additional investigation against the cost of delay
shows willingness to revisit reversible decisions

concepts: breaking an unfamiliar problem into smaller pieces
questionPatterns: What do you do when you're assigned something you have no idea how to implement?
How do you decide when to keep investigating a problem yourself versus asking someone for help?
evaluationCriteria: does not immediately give up or blindly start coding
breaks uncertainty into specific questions
attempts independent investigation before escalating
asks focused questions when blocked

concepts: technical debt as a conscious tradeoff
planning cleanup when shortcuts are necessary
questionPatterns: How do you balance writing clean code with delivering something quickly?
Tell me about a time you intentionally accepted technical debt to meet a deadline.
evaluationCriteria: avoids absolute answers such as quality always winning or speed always winning
distinguishes critical concerns like correctness and security from optional polish
makes technical debt explicit rather than accidental
has a strategy for revisiting deliberate shortcuts

concepts: knowing when to ask for help
questionPatterns: Tell me about a project where you worked with very little supervision.
How do you keep yourself accountable when nobody is checking your progress every day?
evaluationCriteria: demonstrates an actual planning and tracking approach
shows initiative rather than waiting for instructions
communicates important blockers instead of hiding them
balances independence with appropriate escalation

concepts: differences in communication or execution style
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you had to work closely with someone whose working style was very different from yours.
How do you collaborate effectively with someone who communicates or approaches problems differently?
evaluationCriteria: doesn't portray their own style as automatically superior
shows willingness to adapt communication
focuses on shared outcomes
provides a concrete example of finding an effective working arrangement

concepts: raising concerns even under delivery pressure
questionPatterns: What would you do if you were asked to ship something you believed created a serious security or privacy risk?
Tell me how you would handle discovering that your application was exposing sensitive user data.
evaluationCriteria: does not knowingly ignore serious user harm for convenience
raises concerns with clear technical evidence
proposes safer alternatives when possible
understands when escalation is appropriate

topic: Why This Role and Career Motivation
concepts: connection between existing experience and future direction
understanding of what they want to learn
questionPatterns: Why are you interested in this role?
How does this position fit into what you want to do next in your career?
evaluationCriteria: connects motivation to specific aspects of the role
shows a coherent progression from existing experience
identifies meaningful areas for growth
does not rely entirely on compensation, brand name, or generic enthusiasm

topic: Handling a Disagreement With a Client or Stakeholder
concepts: respecting final ownership of business decisions
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time a client wanted something you believed was a bad technical decision.
What would you do if a stakeholder insisted on an approach you strongly disagreed with?
evaluationCriteria: tries to understand the underlying requirement before rejecting the request
communicates tradeoffs in business-relevant terms
offers alternatives rather than simply saying no
knows when to document concerns and commit to the final decision

topic: Measuring the Impact of Your Work
concepts: connecting implementation to user or business value
questionPatterns: How do you know whether something you built was actually successful?
What measurable impact did one of your projects have?
evaluationCriteria: connects technical work to an observable outcome
uses real metrics when available
does not invent numbers when measurements were unavailable
can describe qualitative evidence when quantitative data does not exist
